topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the screen, his breath catching in his throat. The headline, his meticulously crafted piece on urban gardening, was undeniably there, but the byline below it belonged to someone entirely unfamiliar. A slow, creeping coldness began to spread through his chest, tightening his diaphragm until drawing a full breath felt like an effort. He blinked, rereading the text, his eyes darting back and forth between the published words and the name attached to them. "Marcus Thorne." Elias leaned back in his chair, the cheap plastic groaning in protest, and slowly, deliberately, he reached for his phone, his fingers trembling slightly as he scrolled through the original submission emails. A low, almost inaudible sound escaped his lips—a mixture of disbelief and a strange, hollow ache.

[story 2]

When Clara opened the PDF, the world seemed to tilt on its axis. The introduction, the insightful analysis she had poured weeks into, was there, perfectly formatted, but the author credit was wrong. Her jaw went slack. She gripped the edge of her desk so tightly her knuckles turned white. A sharp, involuntary intake of air escaped her lungs, sounding loud in the quiet office. "No," she whispered, the sound thin and reedy. She began rapidly clicking through the website, her movements jerky, searching for an explanation that didn't exist. A flush crept up her neck, staining her cheeks a deep crimson. The realization hit her with the force of a physical blow, leaving her momentarily paralyzed, unable to move or process the sheer absurdity of the situation.

[story 3]

The email notification pinged, and Julian picked up his tablet. His gaze dropped to the article summary. His hand froze halfway to the screen. He reread the attribution twice, his brow furrowing deeply. A sudden, sharp intake of breath hitched in his chest, making his shoulders tense up instantly. He pushed the tablet away from him, the movement abrupt and clumsy. His eyes scanned the page again, searching for any misplaced signature, any clue. A slow, deliberate shake of his head followed, a faint tremor running through his hands as he processed the incongruity of the situation. He leaned forward, resting his elbows on the desk, his expression hardening into something unreadable, yet deeply unsettled.
